Guideline: Define a users table with password_hash and role columns
Description: Store user credentials securely. The password_hash column stores bcrypt output, the role column uses a CHECK constraint, and email has a unique index.
Do: Use BIGSERIAL id, TEXT password_hash with bcrypt, role with CHECK constraint, unique email, timestamps.
Dont: Store plaintext passwords, use integer for roles without constraint, skip email unique index.
Code Bad:
CREATE TABLE users (
    id INT AUTO_INCREMENT,
    email VARCHAR(255),
    password VARCHAR(255),
    role INT DEFAULT 0,
    PRIMARY KEY (id)
);

Guideline: Hash passwords with bcrypt at cost 12
Description: Use golang.org/x/crypto/bcrypt for password hashing. Cost 12 balances security and performance (~250ms per hash). Never store plaintext passwords.
Do: Use bcrypt.GenerateFromPassword with cost 12. Compare with bcrypt.CompareHashAndPassword. Never log or return the hash.
Dont: Store plaintext passwords, use MD5/SHA256 for passwords, use cost lower than 10.

Guideline: Register handler with input validation and password hashing
Description: Validate email format and password strength, hash password with bcrypt, insert user into database, return user without password_hash.
Do: Validate email and password length (min 8 chars), hash before insert, return created user without hash, use proper HTTP status 201.
Dont: Return password_hash in response, skip validation, use GET for registration, allow empty passwords.

Guideline: Login handler with credential verification and JWT generation
Description: Find user by email, compare password with bcrypt, generate JWT with role claims, return token. Use same error message for both wrong email and wrong password to prevent enumeration.
Do: Return same error for invalid email or password, use bcrypt.CompareHashAndPassword, generate JWT with role claim, set reasonable expiry (15min access + 7d refresh).
Dont: Return different errors for wrong email vs wrong password, skip bcrypt comparison, use long-lived access tokens.

Guideline: Generate JWT tokens with role and email claims
Description: Include sub (user ID), role, email, iat, and exp claims. Use HS256 signing with a strong secret. Access token: 15 min expiry. Refresh token: 7 days.
Do: Use jwt.NewWithClaims with MapClaims, include role and email in claims, set short expiry for access tokens, use crypto/rand for secrets.
Dont: Hardcode JWT secret, skip expiry, include password_hash in claims, use none algorithm.
Code Bad:
func GenerateJWT(userID int64) string {
    token := jwt.New(jwt.SigningMethodNone)
    claims := token.Claims.(jwt.MapClaims)
    claims["sub"] = userID
    t, _ := token.SignedString(jwt.UnsafeAllowNoneSignatureType)
    return t
}

Guideline: Validate Bearer token and inject claims into request context
Description: Parse Authorization header, validate JWT signature and expiry, extract claims (userID, role, email), store in context. Return 401 for invalid/expired tokens.
Do: Parse Bearer token from Authorization header, validate with jwt.Parse, store claims in context via context.WithValue, call next handler on success.
Dont: Skip signature validation, ignore token expiry, parse token in every handler instead of middleware.

Guideline: Check admin role from JWT claims in middleware
Description: Dedicated middleware that runs after AuthMiddleware. Extracts claims from context and checks role == admin. Returns 403 Forbidden if not admin. Chain: AuthMiddleware -> AdminOnly -> handler.
Do: Chain AdminOnly after AuthMiddleware, extract claims from context, check role field, return 403 with generic message.
Dont: Check admin role inside every handler, return 401 instead of 403, expose role requirements in error message.

Guideline: Role-based access control with role hierarchy and permission middleware
Description: Define roles as typed constants with a hierarchy (user < admin < superadmin). Create a RequireRole middleware that accepts minimum role level. Superadmin inherits all admin permissions.
Do: Define role constants with iota, create RoleLevel map for hierarchy, use RequireRole(minRole) middleware factory.
Dont: Use string comparison for roles, hardcode role checks, skip role hierarchy.

Guideline: Type-safe helper functions to get auth claims from context
Description: Create helper functions GetClaims, GetUserID, GetUserRole that extract from context with proper type assertion. Use typed context key to avoid collisions.
Do: Use typed ctxKey string constant. Return (value + bool) or (value + error) from helpers. Never use string keys directly.
Dont: Use raw string keys for context, skip type assertion checks, panic on missing claims.
Code Bad: func GetUserID(r *http.Request) int64 {\n    return r.Context().Value("userID").(int64) // panics if missing\n}

Guideline: Issue opaque refresh tokens stored in database with rotation
Description: Generate crypto-random refresh token, store hashed in DB with user_id and expiry. On refresh: validate, delete old token, issue new access + refresh pair. This is token rotation.
Do: Generate 32-byte crypto/rand token. Store SHA256 hash in DB (not plaintext). Rotate on every use. Set 7-day expiry. Delete on logout.
Dont: Store refresh token in plaintext, reuse same token forever, skip rotation, store in localStorage.

Guideline: Delete refresh token on logout to revoke session
Description: On logout, delete the refresh token from database. For JWT access tokens, they remain valid until expiry (short-lived 15min). For stricter revocation, maintain a blacklist.
Do: Delete refresh token from DB on logout. Use short-lived access tokens (15min) so forced revocation is rarely needed. If needed, maintain an in-memory blacklist for JWTs.
Dont: Rely on client to delete token, use long-lived access tokens without revocation, skip refresh token cleanup.
Code Bad: func (h *AuthHandler) Logout(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {\n    w.Write([]byte("bye")) // does nothing server-side\n}

Guideline: Secure password reset flow with expiring token
Description: Generate crypto-random token, store hashed in DB with expiry (1 hour). Send reset link via email. On reset: validate token, hash new password, update user, delete token.
Do: Generate 32-byte random token, store SHA256 hash with 1-hour expiry. Validate token + update password in single transaction. Return same response for valid and invalid emails (prevent enumeration).
Dont: Send password in email, use predictable tokens, long expiry, expose whether email exists.

Guideline: Verify user email with confirmation token
Description: Generate verification token on registration, send via email. Confirm endpoint validates token and sets email_verified = true. Require email verification before allowing sensitive operations.
Do: Generate random token, store hashed with user_id. Send verification email. On confirm: validate token, set email_verified = true, delete token.
Dont: Skip email verification, use predictable tokens, allow unverified users full access.

Guideline: Rate limit login attempts per IP and per email
Description: Use a rate limiter (token bucket or sliding window) on the login endpoint. Limit per IP address (10/min) and per email (5/min) to prevent brute force attacks.
Do: Use golang.org/x/time/rate or a Redis-based limiter. Key by IP + email. Return 429 Too Many Requests. Include Retry-After header.
Dont: Allow unlimited login attempts, only limit by IP (not email), use sleep instead of proper rate limiting.

Guideline: Use constant-time comparison for secrets and tokens
Description: Use crypto/subtle.ConstantTimeCompare for comparing secrets, API keys, and tokens to prevent timing attacks. Timing attacks can leak information about correct characters.
Do: Use subtle.ConstantTimeCompare for all secret comparisons (API keys, HMAC, tokens). Convert strings to []byte first.
Dont: Use == for secret comparison, use strings.Compare, compare character by character.

Guideline: Return generic auth errors to clients and log details internally
Description: Never expose whether an email exists, which role is required, or why a token is invalid. Use generic messages like 'invalid credentials' or 'forbidden'. Log detailed info server-side.
Do: Return generic 401/403 messages. Log the specific reason (expired, invalid signature, wrong role) with structured logging. Same response time for valid and invalid attempts.
Dont: Return 'user not found' vs 'wrong password', expose required role in error, leak token validation details.

Guideline: Set secure cookie attributes for session tokens
Description: When using cookie-based sessions, set HttpOnly (no JS access), Secure (HTTPS only), SameSite=Strict (CSRF protection), and reasonable Max-Age.
Do: Set HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict, Path=/. Use short Max-Age for session cookies. Domain-scope appropriately.
Dont: Skip HttpOnly (allows XSS to steal cookies), skip Secure (sent over HTTP), use SameSite=None without Secure.
Code Good:
http.SetCookie(w, &http.Cookie{
    Name:     "session_token",
    Value:    token,
    Path:     "/",
    MaxAge:   3600, // 1 hour
    HttpOnly: true,
    Secure:   true,
    SameSite: http.SameSiteStrictMode,
})

Guideline: Implement Google or GitHub OAuth2 login flow
Description: Use golang.org/x/oauth2 for the authorization code flow. Redirect user to provider, exchange code for token, fetch user info, create or link account.
Do: Use oauth2.Config for provider setup. Validate state parameter to prevent CSRF. Exchange code server-side. Create user if not exists.
Dont: Store client secret in frontend, skip state validation, trust user-provided email without verifying with provider.

Guideline: Test auth middleware and login flow with httptest
Description: Use httptest.NewServer or httptest.NewRecorder to test auth endpoints. Create helper to generate valid JWT for tests. Test both authenticated and unauthenticated paths.
Do: Create test helpers for JWT generation. Test middleware with valid/expired/malformed tokens. Use table-driven tests for login scenarios. Mock the user repository.
Dont: Test only the happy path, skip expired token tests, use real database in unit tests.

Guideline: Organize routes into public and protected groups with chi
Description: Use chi.Route groups to separate public auth routes (/auth/register, /auth/login) from protected API routes (/api/*) and admin routes (/admin/*). Apply middleware at the group level.
Do: Use r.Group or r.Route to organize. Apply AuthMiddleware to /api group. Apply AuthMiddleware + AdminOnly to /admin group. Keep /auth routes public.
Dont: Apply auth middleware globally (blocks register/login), mix public and protected routes without groups.
Code Bad:
r := chi.NewRouter()
r.Use(AuthMiddleware(secret)) // blocks /register and /login!
r.Post("/register", authHandler.Register)
r.Post("/login", authHandler.Login)
r.Get("/admin/users", adminHandler.ListUsers) // no admin check!
